bytes.topic: Warehouse vs Database — OLAP vs OLTP, columnar vs row storage, analytical workloads
bytes.title: Databricks SQL Warehouses — OLAP-First Design and Why You Shouldn't Replace Postgres
bytes.body:
Delta Lake stores data as Parquet files — columnar format. Reads scan only requested columns, not full rows, making aggregations across billions of events extremely efficient. Databricks SQL Warehouses run on Photon, a vectorized C++ engine purpose-built for this columnar layout.

This is fundamentally OLAP territory. Delta is not a replacement for Postgres or MySQL. While Delta supports ACID transactions via its transaction log, DELETE and UPDATE are file-rewrite operations — not in-place row mutations. Running thousands of single-row writes per second against a SQL Warehouse creates small-file debt and compaction pressure fast.

Your primary performance lever for analytical queries is OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY — it co-locates related data across Parquet files, enabling Databricks' file-level data skipping to eliminate irrelevant files at query time. This replaces traditional B-tree indexes.

SQL Warehouse type matters too: serverless scales to zero for ad-hoc workloads; classic pro/enterprise clusters sustain heavy ETL pipelines. For transactional application backends, keep your row-store database. Let Databricks own the analytical tier.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Optimize Delta table for analytical scans (OLAP pattern)
OPTIMIZE silver.user_events
ZORDER BY (user_id, event_date);

-- Photon-accelerated columnar scan with file-level data skipping
SELECT
  user_id,
  DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date) AS month,
  COUNT(*)                        AS event_count
FROM silver.user_events
WHERE event_date >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY 2 DESC;

-- Anti-pattern: high-frequency single-row MERGE = repeated file rewrites
-- Use batch COPY INTO or micro-batch Structured Streaming instead

bytes.topic: Snowflake — virtual warehouses, zero-copy cloning, time travel, data sharing
bytes.title: Databricks Equivalents to Snowflake's Four Core Features: Warehouses, Cloning, Time Travel & Sharing
bytes.body:
Snowflake's flagship features map directly to Databricks primitives — and Delta Lake often extends them further.

**Virtual Warehouses → SQL Warehouses:** Databricks SQL Warehouses (serverless or pro tier) auto-scale like Snowflake's multi-cluster warehouses. Key difference: all warehouses share one Delta Lake storage layer, eliminating the per-warehouse result cache divergence Snowflake users work around.

**Zero-Copy Cloning → `SHALLOW CLONE`:** Snowflake clones reference original micropartitions. Databricks' `SHALLOW CLONE` creates a metadata-only pointer via the Delta transaction log. Writes to the clone generate new Parquet files without touching source data — same semantic, open format.

**Time Travel → Delta Time Travel:** Both use point-in-time queries, but Databricks exposes `VERSION AS OF` (by transaction ID) and `TIMESTAMP AS OF`. Retention defaults to 30 days, tunable via `delta.logRetentionDuration`. `DESCRIBE HISTORY` gives full audit lineage — no Snowflake equivalent for transaction-level inspection.

**Data Sharing → Delta Sharing:** Snowflake sharing locks both sides into Snowflake. Delta Sharing is an open protocol — external consumers query live Delta tables over HTTPS from Spark, pandas, Power BI, or DuckDB with no Databricks account required.

bytes.topic: BigQuery — serverless model, slot reservations, partitioning, clustering, cost control
bytes.title: Databricks + BigQuery — Pulling Data Without Blowing Your Slot Budget
bytes.body: When Databricks reads from BigQuery via the Spark-BigQuery connector, every query hits BigQuery's compute model — either on-demand (per-TB scanned) or reserved slots. Understanding this prevents surprise bills. BigQuery partitioning and clustering are your primary cost levers: a partitioned table on `event_date` lets BigQuery prune entire partition blocks before scanning; clustering on high-cardinality columns like `user_id` further colocates data so range filters skip irrelevant micro-partitions. Critically, Spark pushdown predicates are honored by the connector — but only for partition filters and simple column predicates. Complex Spark expressions (UDFs, non-sargable conditions) do NOT push down, causing full table scans regardless of your BigQuery clustering. To control slot consumption from Databricks jobs, always pass `.option("filter", "...")` explicitly rather than relying on Spark's lazy filter pushdown. For recurring pipelines, prefer BigQuery slot reservations over on-demand — a 100-slot baseline reservation is dramatically cheaper than repeated on-demand scans of multi-TB tables. Use `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS` in BigQuery to audit which Databricks jobs consumed the most slots.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Databricks notebook — cost-efficient BigQuery read
df = (
    spark.read
    .format("bigquery")
    .option("parentProject", "my-gcp-project")
    .option("table", "my_project.analytics.events")
    # Explicit partition filter — guarantees pushdown, avoids full scan
    .option("filter", "event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'")
    .option("createDisposition", "CREATE_NEVER")
    .load()
)

# Project early — reduces bytes transferred to Databricks cluster
(
    df.select("user_id", "event_type", "event_date", "revenue_usd")
    .write.format("delta")
    .partitionBy("event_date")
    .saveAsTable("main.bronze.bq_events")
)

bytes.topic: Redshift — distribution keys, sort keys, VACUUM, ANALYZE, RA3 nodes
bytes.title: Databricks — Redshift Concepts Mapped to Delta Lake (Distribution, Sort Keys, VACUUM)
bytes.body: If you're migrating from Redshift or reading its docs, the mental model differs sharply from Delta Lake. Redshift's distribution keys control data co-location across slices — in Databricks, you get this via `PARTITIONED BY` on high-cardinality columns or `DISTRIBUTE BY` in Spark SQL joins. Redshift compound/interleaved sort keys map to Delta's `ZORDER BY` in `OPTIMIZE` commands — Z-ordering clusters correlated columns together using space-filling curves, unlike Redshift's sequential sort. For Redshift's `VACUUM` (reclaims space after deletes/updates) + `ANALYZE` (refreshes column stats), Delta Lake splits this into two explicit operations: `VACUUM` removes unreferenced Parquet files older than the retention threshold (default 7 days), while `ANALYZE TABLE` regenerates column-level statistics the optimizer uses for predicate pushdown and join reordering. Redshift RA3 nodes decouple compute from managed storage (S3) — Databricks operates the same way natively via cloud object storage, so you never size storage separately. Use `OPTIMIZE` + `ZORDER` together on frequently-filtered columns post-ingestion; skip both on append-only event tables where full scans dominate.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Databricks SQL equivalents of Redshift maintenance operations

-- Equivalent of Redshift VACUUM SORT ONLY (reorganize + Z-order clustering)
OPTIMIZE catalog.schema.events
ZORDER BY (user_id, event_date);

-- Equivalent of Redshift ANALYZE (refresh column stats for query optimizer)
ANALYZE TABLE catalog.schema.events
COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS;

-- Equivalent of Redshift VACUUM DELETE ONLY (purge stale Delta versions)
VACUUM catalog.schema.events RETAIN 168 HOURS;

-- Equivalent of Redshift distribution key (partition for co-location)
CREATE TABLE catalog.schema.events (
  user_id BIGINT,
  event_date DATE,
  payload STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date)
USING DELTA;

bytes.topic: Databricks — Delta Lake, Unity Catalog, notebooks, Spark integration, Photon engine
bytes.title: Databricks Delta Lake — Incremental CDC with Change Data Feed
bytes.body:
Delta Lake's Change Data Feed (CDF) exposes row-level change operations — insert, update_preimage, update_postimage, delete — without full table scans. Enable it once per table and every subsequent write appends a change log that downstream jobs can read incrementally.

This is the backbone of low-latency CDC pipelines in Databricks. Instead of diffing snapshots, you read only what changed since a given version or timestamp. `update_postimage` gives you the committed row state after an update; `update_preimage` gives the before-state — useful for audit trails or GDPR right-to-erasure verification.

CDF pairs natively with Structured Streaming: set `readChangeFeed` to `true` on a streaming reader and Databricks automatically tracks the latest processed version in the checkpoint. Photon accelerates the underlying Delta log replay, so high-churn tables stay fast.

Avoid enabling CDF on append-only tables — the overhead is real and the change log grows with every write. Use `DESCRIBE HISTORY` to inspect version numbers before setting `startingVersion` in production jobs.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Enable CDF on an existing Unity Catalog table
spark.sql("""
  ALTER TABLE catalog.schema.user_events
  SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)
""")

# Batch read: changes since version 12
changes = (
    spark.read
        .format("delta")
        .option("readChangeFeed", "true")
        .option("startingVersion", 12)
        .table("catalog.schema.user_events")
)

# Keep only post-update and insert rows
changes.filter(
    "_change_type IN ('insert', 'update_postimage')"
).select("user_id", "event_type", "_commit_timestamp").show()

bytes.topic: Data Modeling — star schema, snowflake schema, wide tables, dimensional modeling
bytes.title: Databricks Data Modeling — Star Schema vs Wide Tables in the Gold Layer
bytes.body:
In Databricks, wide tables typically outperform star schemas in the gold layer. Delta Lake's columnar Parquet storage eliminates the join-overhead argument for normalization — Photon's vectorized engine scans wide tables without the shuffle cost of multi-table joins. Star and snowflake schemas still win when dimensions are shared across multiple fact tables; use Unity Catalog to register those dimension tables as governed assets across workspaces and catalogs.

For star schemas, ZORDER BY on your primary join keys co-locates matching data files, cutting file scans on JOIN predicates by 60–80% in practice. Schedule OPTIMIZE via Databricks Workflows to keep Z-ordering effective after incremental loads.

For wide (denormalized) tables, partition by a high-cardinality time column (e.g., event_date) and ZORDER on your most-filtered attribute (e.g., region). Avoid partitioning on low-cardinality columns like status — Delta's file skipping already handles those efficiently.

Before committing to either strategy, run EXPLAIN and DESCRIBE DETAIL in Databricks SQL to confirm file skipping is actually reducing scan size under your real query patterns.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Star schema: fact table optimized for dimension joins
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE gold.fact_orders
USING DELTA PARTITIONED BY (order_date)
AS SELECT * FROM silver.orders;

OPTIMIZE gold.fact_orders ZORDER BY (customer_id, product_id);

-- Wide table: pre-joined for BI/dashboard workloads
CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE gold.wide_orders
USING DELTA PARTITIONED BY (order_date)
AS
SELECT o.*, c.region, c.segment, p.category, p.brand
FROM silver.orders o
JOIN silver.customers c USING (customer_id)
JOIN silver.products  p USING (product_id);

OPTIMIZE gold.wide_orders ZORDER BY (region, category);

bytes.topic: ELT vs ETL — why modern warehouses favor ELT, dbt as the transformation layer
bytes.title: Databricks ELT with dbt — Stop Transforming Before You Load
bytes.body:
ETL made sense when warehouses were slow and storage was expensive. Databricks inverts both assumptions: Photon-accelerated SQL and cheap Delta Lake object storage mean transforming inside the warehouse is faster and cheaper than pre-processing before landing.

The pattern: land raw data into a Bronze Delta table via Auto Loader (`cloudFiles` format) or `COPY INTO`, then delegate the T to dbt running against a Databricks SQL warehouse via the `dbt-databricks` adapter. Bronze stays immutable — append-only, schema-on-read. dbt handles Silver and Gold as materialized Delta tables.

The `dbt-databricks` adapter defaults to `file_format='delta'` and supports three-level Unity Catalog namespaces (`catalog.schema.model`) natively in `dbt_project.yml`. Incremental models use Delta's `MERGE` under the hood when `incremental_strategy='merge'` is set — no Spark job, no orchestration glue, just SQL pushed to Photon.

This eliminates a whole class of failure: no serialization between pipeline stages, no schema drift between transform and load, and Unity Catalog lineage traces directly from Bronze source to Gold output without manual tagging.

bytes.topic: Partitioning & Clustering — partition pruning, query performance, partition strategy
bytes.title: Databricks Partitioning & Clustering — Stop Scanning Tables You Don't Need To
bytes.body:
Delta Lake partition pruning eliminates entire file directories before a single row is read — but only when your filter column matches the partition column exactly. Partitioning on high-cardinality columns like `user_id` creates millions of small files (the small-file problem), tanking both write and read performance. Reserve partitioning for low-cardinality, frequently-filtered columns like `event_date` or `region`.

For high-cardinality filtering, use Liquid Clustering (`CLUSTER BY`) instead — introduced in Databricks Runtime 13.3. It uses Z-order-style co-location without static directory partitions, adapts to changing query patterns via `OPTIMIZE`, and avoids partition explosion entirely.

Check whether pruning is actually firing: in the Spark UI, a pruned scan shows `numFiles` far below total — if it equals total, your predicate isn't aligning with partition boundaries. Watch for implicit casts (`date_trunc` on a `DATE` partition column) silently disabling pruning. Always match filter expression type to the partition column type precisely.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Legacy: static date partitioning
CREATE TABLE events (
  event_id STRING,
  user_id STRING,
  payload STRING
) USING DELTA
PARTITIONED BY (event_date DATE);

-- Modern: Liquid Clustering (DBR 13.3+) — no partition explosion
CREATE TABLE events (
  event_id STRING,
  user_id STRING,
  event_date DATE,
  payload STRING
) USING DELTA
CLUSTER BY (user_id, event_date);

-- Incrementally recluster after bulk writes
OPTIMIZE events;

bytes.topic: Cost Management — query cost estimation, slot/credit consumption, materialized views
bytes.title: Databricks Cost Management — Query Estimation, DBU Consumption, and Materialized Views
bytes.body:
Databricks bills in DBUs (Databricks Units) — every SQL warehouse query consumes them based on warehouse size (2X-Small to 4X-Large) and runtime. Run EXPLAIN COST before executing multi-table joins on large Delta tables; it surfaces plan cardinalities and shuffle operations that are the primary DBU drivers, even without giving a direct DBU estimate.

For recurring aggregations powering dashboards or BI tools, Databricks Materialized Views (Unity Catalog required) are the highest-leverage cost lever. Unlike standard views that re-execute on every query hit, MVs persist computed results and refresh incrementally using Delta log checkpointing — dramatically reducing scanned rows and DBU spend per query.

Audit actual consumption via system.billing.usage (SKU, DBU quantity, workspace ID) and cross-reference with system.query.history to pinpoint expensive query patterns. Serverless SQL warehouses eliminate idle DBU burn by scaling to zero between queries. Complement with ZORDER on high-cardinality filter columns to minimize file scans, and enforce cluster policies with DBU-per-hour caps to prevent runaway spend on shared workspaces.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Materialize expensive aggregation (requires Unity Catalog)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW catalog.schema.daily_event_counts
AS
SELECT event_date, event_type, COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM catalog.schema.events
GROUP BY event_date, event_type;

-- Trigger incremental refresh from Delta log
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW catalog.schema.daily_event_counts;

-- Audit DBU burn by SKU over the last 7 days
SELECT sku_name, SUM(usage_quantity) AS total_dbus
FROM system.billing.usage
WHERE usage_date >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, CURRENT_DATE())
GROUP BY sku_name ORDER BY total_dbus DESC;

bytes.topic: Data Quality — null handling, deduplication, constraint enforcement in warehouses
bytes.title: Databricks Data Quality — Null Handling, Deduplication, and Constraints at Scale
bytes.body: Delta Lake's CHECK constraints and NOT NULL constraints enforce data quality at write time — any violating row raises an AnalysisException and aborts the transaction, keeping bad data out of your table entirely. Define them with ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT valid_status CHECK (status IN ('active', 'inactive')). For null handling in ETL, use DataFrame.na.fill() with column-specific defaults or coalesce() in SQL to substitute sentinel values before writing. Deduplication is best handled with MERGE INTO using a deterministic key — match on composite keys, update on conflict, insert on new. For streaming deduplication, enable dropDuplicates() on a watermarked column before the sink write, combined with a trigger.availableNow() for batch-style micro-batch control. Unity Catalog adds a layer via table-level data quality expectations through Lakehouse Monitoring, letting you define freshness, volume, and custom metric rules that surface anomalies in the system.information_schema. Combine constraints + MERGE + Monitoring for a defense-in-depth quality pipeline.

bytes.topic: Access Control — role-based access, column-level security, row-level policies
bytes.title: Databricks Unity Catalog — Row Filters, Column Masks, and RBAC Done Right
bytes.body:
Unity Catalog is Databricks' single governance layer across all workspaces. Access control operates at three levels: catalog, schema, and table — each granting privileges to users, service principals, or groups via GRANT statements.

For row-level security, Unity Catalog uses row filter functions. You define a SQL function that returns a boolean, then bind it to a table with ALTER TABLE ... SET ROW FILTER. At query time, Databricks injects the filter automatically — no view wrappers needed. The function can call IS_MEMBER() or CURRENT_USER() to scope rows per principal.

Column-level security uses column masks, similarly bound via ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK. The mask function receives the column value and returns either the real value or a redacted version based on group membership. Unlike traditional VIEWs, masks and filters survive table renames and are enforced even on Delta sharing exports.

For RBAC, avoid granting directly to users — always use groups synced from your IdP via SCIM. Combine with data owner patterns: catalog admins own schemas, team leads own tables, analysts get SELECT only.

bytes.topic: Performance Optimization — result caching, materialized views, query optimization
bytes.title: Databricks Query Performance — Delta Caching, Materialized Views, and AQE
bytes.body: Databricks offers three distinct caching layers that most engineers conflate. Delta Cache (disk-based, automatic on DBR clusters) decompresses and stores Parquet data locally — it's not the same as Spark's in-memory `CACHE TABLE`. Enable it explicitly with `spark.databricks.io.cache.enabled true` and size it via `spark.databricks.io.cache.maxDiskUsage`. For repeated aggregations, Databricks SQL Materialized Views persist results and refresh incrementally on Delta tables using `CREATE OR REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW`. Unlike standard views, they push computation to refresh time, not query time — critical for BI workloads hitting the same rollup repeatedly. Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is on by default in DBR 7.3+: it re-optimizes mid-flight based on shuffle partition statistics. Force broadcast joins for small tables with `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold 10485760`. For skewed joins, `spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled true` splits hot partitions automatically. Profile bottlenecks via the Spark UI's SQL tab — look for `PhotonShuffleExchangeSink` stages; those are your rewrite targets.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Create a Databricks SQL materialized view for daily active users
CREATE OR REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.daily_active_users
AS
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('day', event_time) AS event_date,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)       AS dau
FROM silver.events
WHERE event_type = 'session_start'
GROUP BY 1;

-- Force AQE skew join handling and cap broadcast threshold
SET spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true;
SET spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold = 10485760; -- 10 MB

-- Verify Delta Cache is active on the cluster
SET spark.databricks.io.cache.enabled = true;

bytes.topic: Data Sharing — cross-account sharing, data marketplace, secure views
bytes.title: Databricks Delta Sharing — Cross-Account Data Without Copying
bytes.body: Delta Sharing is Databricks' open protocol for sharing live data across accounts, clouds, and even non-Databricks recipients — no data duplication, no ETL pipelines. On the provider side, you create a Share, add tables or partitions, then grant it to a Recipient backed by either Unity Catalog (for Databricks-to-Databricks) or a token-based profile (for open clients). Recipients query data directly against your Delta Lake — they always see the latest version without you pushing anything. For sensitive columns, use secure views: define a view in your metastore that masks or filters rows, then share the view instead of the base table. Unity Catalog enforces column-level and row-level filters transparently. On the Databricks Marketplace, you can publish a Share as a listing — consumers discover and mount it in one click via `CREATE CATALOG USING SHARE`. No S3 bucket policies, no cross-account IAM roles, no scheduled exports. Access is revocable instantly by dropping the recipient or rotating its activation link.

bytes.topic: Incremental Loading — append vs upsert strategies, CDC, merge patterns
bytes.title: Databricks Incremental Loading — Append vs Upsert and When to Use Each
bytes.body:
Delta Lake gives you two primary incremental patterns. Append-only works when source records are immutable — structured streaming with `trigger(availableNow=True)` writes new rows directly, zero merge overhead. Use `COPY INTO` for batch file ingestion with automatic deduplication via `COPY_OPTIONS ('mergeSchema' = 'true')`.

For mutable sources, Delta's `MERGE INTO` handles upserts atomically. Pair it with CDC feeds from Debezium or DMS: filter `op = 'D'` rows for deletes, `op = 'U'`/`'I'` for upserts. The merge evaluates on matched/not-matched clauses in a single scan — no separate delete pass.

Delta Live Tables abstracts this further: declare a `STREAMING TABLE` with `apply_changes()` to handle CDC automatically, respecting sequence ordering via a `sequence_by` column to resolve late-arriving events.

Performance tip: Z-ORDER your merge key columns and set `delta.targetFileSize` to reduce small file amplification on high-frequency upsert workloads. Use `OPTIMIZE` + `VACUUM` post-merge to reclaim storage from superseded file versions.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- CDC upsert pattern using Delta MERGE
MERGE INTO silver.customers AS target
USING (
  SELECT * FROM bronze.customers_cdc
  WHERE op IN ('I', 'U', 'D')
) AS source
ON target.customer_id = source.customer_id
WHEN MATCHED AND source.op = 'D' THEN DELETE
WHEN MATCHED AND source.op = 'U' THEN
  UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND source.op = 'I' THEN
  INSERT *;

bytes.topic: Semi-structured Data — JSON/Parquet handling, schema evolution, nested types
bytes.title: Databricks Delta Lake — Taming JSON and Schema Evolution with Semi-Structured Data
bytes.body:
Databricks natively handles JSON ingestion via `cloudFiles` with Auto Loader, but the real power comes from combining `VARIANT` type (DBR 15+) with Delta Lake's schema evolution. For nested JSON, `from_json()` with an explicit schema beats `schema_of_json()` inference on production data — inference scans the entire dataset and misses nulls in sparse fields.

For Parquet with evolving schemas, enable `mergeSchema` at the write level or set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` cluster-wide. Delta tracks schema changes in its transaction log, so time-travel queries (`VERSION AS OF`) still respect the original schema at that point.

For deeply nested structs, use `schema_of_json()` once offline to generate a baseline, then hardcode it. Explode arrays with `explode_outer()` (not `explode`) to preserve rows with empty arrays — a silent data-loss footgun in ETL pipelines. Use `posexplode` when order within the array carries semantic meaning.
bytes.code_snippet:
from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, explode_outer, col
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, ArrayType

event_schema = StructType([
    StructField("user_id", StringType()),
    StructField("tags", ArrayType(StringType())),
    StructField("meta", StructType([
        StructField("source", StringType()),
        StructField("version", StringType())
    ]))
])

df = spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles") \
    .option("cloudFiles.format", "json") \
    .option("cloudFiles.schemaLocation", "/mnt/checkpoints/schema") \
    .load("/mnt/raw/events")

parsed = df.withColumn("data", from_json(col("value"), event_schema)) \
    .select("data.*")

flattened = parsed.select(
    col("user_id"),
    col("meta.source").alias("source"),
    explode_outer(col("tags")).alias("tag")
)

flattened.writeStream.format("delta") \
    .option("mergeSchema", "true") \
    .option("checkpointLocation", "/mnt/checkpoints/events") \
    .toTable("catalog.silver.events")

bytes.topic: Monitoring — query history, cost dashboards, slow query alerts
bytes.title: Databricks Monitoring — Query History, Cost Dashboards, and Slow Query Alerts
bytes.body:
Databricks exposes full query telemetry through `system.query.history`, a Unity Catalog system table populated within minutes of execution. Every SQL warehouse run lands here: duration, bytes scanned, queued time, user, and status. For slow query alerts, create a Databricks SQL Alert against a query filtering `total_duration_ms > 30000` and `status = 'FINISHED'`, schedule it every 15 minutes, and route to Slack or PagerDuty. Avoid alerting on `RUNNING` rows — propagation lag causes false positives on in-flight queries.

For cost dashboards, join `system.query.history` with `system.billing.usage` on `warehouse_id` and `usage_date` to correlate DBU spend per query pattern or user. Pin a Databricks SQL serverless dashboard with a bar chart of daily DBU from `system.billing.usage` grouped by `sku_name` and `workspace_id`. Per-user attribution uses `executed_by` from query history joined to billing on warehouse and date.

For deep query inspection, enable Query Profiles in SQL Admin Console settings — this gives EXPLAIN-level operator trees and spill metrics for any historical statement without re-execution. Combine with `total_task_duration_ms` to identify CPU-bound vs. I/O-bound bottlenecks.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Identify slow queries via Databricks system.query.history (Unity Catalog)
SELECT
  executed_by,
  warehouse_id,
  ROUND(total_duration_ms   / 1000.0, 1) AS duration_sec,
  ROUND(total_task_duration_ms / 1000.0, 1) AS cpu_sec,
  LEFT(statement_text, 120)              AS query_preview
FROM system.query.history
WHERE
  start_time       >= DATEADD(DAY, -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
  AND status        = 'FINISHED'
  AND total_duration_ms > 30000
ORDER BY total_duration_ms DESC
LIMIT 25;

bytes.topic: Integration — connecting to dbt, Airflow, BI tools, reverse ETL
bytes.title: Databricks as an Integration Hub — dbt, Airflow, BI Tools, and Reverse ETL
bytes.body: Databricks sits at the center of the modern data stack, exposing clean integration points at every layer. For dbt, use the `dbt-databricks` adapter (not `dbt-spark`) — it supports Unity Catalog three-part naming (`catalog.schema.model`) and pushes compute to a SQL Warehouse via HTTP path, not a cluster. For Airflow, `DatabricksRunNowOperator` triggers existing Jobs by `job_id`, while `DatabricksSubmitRunOperator` submits ad-hoc task specs — both authenticate via Personal Access Token or OAuth M2M stored in an Airflow connection. BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Looker) connect through Databricks SQL Warehouse JDBC/ODBC endpoints; Partner Connect auto-provisions these with correct driver versions. For reverse ETL (Census, Hightouch), point the source at a SQL Warehouse endpoint and grant `SELECT` on Unity Catalog tables — avoid direct cluster connections since warehouses auto-suspend and scale independently. Delta Sharing is the zero-copy alternative when the destination supports it, skipping the ETL layer entirely.
bytes.code_snippet:
# profiles.yml — dbt-databricks (not dbt-spark)
my_project:
  target: prod
  outputs:
    prod:
      type: databricks
      catalog: main          # Unity Catalog catalog
      schema: analytics
      host: adb-<workspace-id>.azuredatabricks.net
      http_path: /sql/1.0/warehouses/<warehouse-id>
      token: "{{ env_var('DBT_DATABRICKS_TOKEN') }}"
      threads: 8

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Snowflake vs BigQuery vs Redshift vs Databricks decision framework
bytes.title: Databricks — A Decision Framework for Choosing It Over Snowflake, BigQuery, or Redshift
bytes.body:
Databricks wins when your workload crosses the ML/data engineering boundary. If your pipeline ends at a dashboard, Snowflake or BigQuery will serve you with less operational overhead. The calculus shifts the moment you need Delta Lake's ACID transactions on open Parquet, MLflow experiment tracking, or Feature Store to share features between training and serving.

Choose Databricks when:
- You run Structured Streaming into Delta Live Tables for declarative, auto-scaling pipelines
- Unity Catalog governs both your BI tables and ML feature tables under one ACL — no separate governance plane
- Photon-accelerated SQL Warehouses serve analysts on the same Delta tables your PySpark jobs write — zero data movement or format conversion
- Delta Sharing lets you publish data externally without forcing consumers to acquire a Snowflake seat

Choose Snowflake when your team is SQL-native and ML happens in a separate system. BigQuery if you're serverless-first on GCP with pay-per-query economics. Redshift if you're deep in AWS and rely on Spectrum for S3 federation.

The tell: if an MLflow run and a Databricks SQL dashboard will ever share the same Unity Catalog table, Databricks is your control plane.
